narrative: As an Asian male applying to law school, your background includes part-time enrollment plans, a family from a lower income bracket, an undergraduate GPA of 2.60—which is below the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 27, notably lower than the average of around 37. Your Year 1 school decile ranking of 2 and Year 3 ranking of 1 also sit well below the dataset averages of about 5.7 and 5.5, respectively, reflecting grades in the lower percentiles compared to other students. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students' academic records, family details, and bar exam outcomes, predicted a 41% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first try. This elevated risk threshold led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger signals of first-time bar passage. Among all the factors considered, your family income had the strongest positive influence, actually pushing the prediction toward success and bar passage, likely because students from similar lower-income backgrounds in the data often showed resilience and high motivation. However, your Year 3 decile ranking exerted the next biggest pull in the opposite direction, significantly increasing the model's concern about bar failure due to its low standing relative to peers. Your LSAT score followed as the third most influential factor, also nudging the prediction toward failure because of how far it trailed the typical scores of students who passed. Overall, while your family circumstances offered a helpful counterbalance, the combination of weaker academic deciles and LSAT performance tipped the scales against you compared to the profiles of admitted students, who generally rank higher in these areas. This doesn't define your potential—many in similar situations have succeeded by targeting improvements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your recent grades to improve your decile rankings, and exploring programs that value part-time students from diverse backgrounds like yours. Building a narrative around your resilience as an Asian male from a modest family could also shine in future applications, perhaps with added test prep or relevant experience. Keep pushing; targeted steps like these have turned around many applications just like yours.
